---
title: "The Korean War & The Incheon Landing"
subtitle: "A Brief Historical Overview"
author: "DJG"
date: "10/12/25"
lightbox: true
format:
  html:
    theme: lux
    toc: true
---

## The Korean War (1950-1953)

The Korean War was a devastating conflict that shaped modern Korea and remains technically unresolved today—the two Koreas are still separated by an armistice, not a peace treaty.

### Origins of the Conflict

**Division of Korea (1945)** - At the end of World War II, Korea was liberated from Japanese colonial rule - The peninsula was divided at the 38th parallel: Soviet forces occupied the north, American forces the south - This temporary division became permanent as Cold War tensions escalated - Two separate governments formed: communist North Korea (DPRK) under Kim Il-sung, and South Korea (ROK) under Syngman Rhee.

**The Invasion (June 25, 1950)** - North Korean forces crossed the 38th parallel and invaded South Korea - The attack was sudden and overwhelming, with Soviet-supplied tanks and weapons - South Korean and initial US forces were pushed back rapidly - Within weeks, UN/South Korean forces were trapped in a small perimeter around the port city of Pusan (now Busan) in the southeast corner.

### The Pusan Perimeter Crisis

By August 1950, the situation was desperate: - North Korean forces controlled 90% of the Korean peninsula - UN forces (primarily American and South Korean) held only a small defensive perimeter around Pusan - If Pusan fell, UN forces would be pushed into the sea - The war appeared nearly lost

## The Incheon Landing: MacArthur's Masterstroke

### The Audacious Plan

General Douglas MacArthur, commander of UN forces, proposed a daring amphibious assault at Incheon, a port city on Korea's western coast near Seoul.

**Why Incheon was considered impossible:** - Extreme tidal variations (30+ feet) - among the highest in the world - Narrow, winding approach channels that could trap ships - Only a few days per month when tides allowed landing - Heavily fortified and far behind enemy lines - Most military planners called it a suicide mission

**Why MacArthur chose it anyway:** - It was precisely *because* it seemed impossible that North Korean forces were unprepared - Success would cut North Korean supply lines in half - Seoul (the capital) was only 20 miles away - It would trap North Korean forces between two fronts.

### Operation Chromite (September 15, 1950)

**The Landing** - 75,000 troops and 261 naval vessels assembled in secret - The landing began at dawn on September 15, 1950 - Marines had to scale seawalls using ladders because of extreme tides - Initial resistance was lighter than expected—the surprise was complete - Within hours, Incheon was secured

**Immediate Impact** - North Korean forces were caught completely off guard - Their supply lines from the north to the Pusan front were severed - UN forces broke out of the Pusan perimeter simultaneously - North Korean army found itself surrounded and began collapsing.

**Liberation of Seoul** - By September 26, Seoul was recaptured after intense street fighting - North Korean forces fled northward in disarray - The war's momentum completely reversed in just 11 days

### The Turning Point

The Incheon landing is considered one of the most brilliant military operations in modern history: - Transformed a near-defeat into sudden victory - Saved South Korea from complete conquest - Demonstrated MacArthur's strategic genius (and considerable ego) - Changed the entire course of the war.

## What Happened Next

**UN Advance North (Fall 1950)** - Emboldened by success, UN forces pushed north past the 38th parallel - By October, they were approaching the Chinese border - MacArthur believed the war was nearly won.

**Chinese Intervention (November 1950)** - China warned against approaching its border but was ignored - Hundreds of thousands of Chinese "volunteers" entered the war - UN forces were driven back south in bitter winter fighting - Seoul changed hands again.

**Stalemate (1951-1953)** - The front lines stabilized roughly along the 38th parallel - Trench warfare and negotiations dragged on for two more years - MacArthur was fired by President Truman in April 1951 for insubordination (he wanted to expand the war into China) - Armistice signed July 27, 1953—but no peace treaty.

## Legacy and Impact

**Human Cost** - Approximately 3 million Korean civilians killed - 1+ million combined military casualties - Entire cities destroyed, families separated - Korea remains divided today along the DMZ (Demilitarized Zone).

**Modern Significance** - The war created the world's most heavily fortified border - North and South Korea developed into radically different societies - Millions of Korean families remain separated - The "Forgotten War" in America, but defining trauma in Korea - Technically still at war—only an armistice, not peace.

**Visiting Historical Sites Today** - **Incheon Landing Memorial Hall** - Museum and monuments at the landing site - **Freedom Bridge** - Symbol of the divided peninsula - **War Memorial of Korea** (Seoul) - Comprehensive museum - **DMZ Tours** - Visit the demilitarized zone separating North and South.

## Why This Matters for Your Visit

Understanding the Korean War helps contextualize modern Korea: - Why Korea is divided - The generation that rebuilt South Korea from ashes - Korea's relationship with the United States - The ongoing tensions with North Korea - The resilience and determination of Korean people - Why military service is mandatory for Korean men.

The Incheon landing, in particular, represents a moment when history pivoted—when catastrophic defeat transformed into unexpected victory, shaping the Korean peninsula for decades to come.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*The Korean War is sometimes called "The Forgotten War" in the West, but for Koreans, it remains the defining event of modern history. Every family has a story from the war.*
